“Up and at ’em!” — pursuit pilots ready to fight
These boys from many
nations escaped the
Nasis. But they will
go back — with bombs
The boys are sitting around the barracks
at a Canadian airfield. All of them wear
the flying blue of the RAF. But they are
not English — they are the United Nations:
The Frenchman was taken prisoner by the
Nazis at Dunkirk. The Dutchman’s ack-ack
battery shot down seven German planes during
the invasion of Holland. The Norwegian was an
army ski trooper who got away from the
Gestapo. The Belgian escaped across the
English channel in a fishing boat.
From All the World
They and their colleagues, brought together
from more than 20 nations by the British
Commonwealth Air Training Plan, have turned
Canada into what President Roosevelt calls
the "Airdrome of Democracy.” The IViminion
has 93 Air Force training schools. The fighting
exiles from conquered countries train with
fledglings from Great Britain, New Zealand,
Australia, Canada. Most members of the flying
foreign legion have seen action and are living
now only for another crack at Hitler. Many of
them are registered under aliases — they
fear reprisals for families still in con
quered countries.
They make grim, determined students. They
don't laugh easily, and they don’t like to talk
about their past. But they are friendly, eager
to learn. As soon as their training, paid for by
the British Empire, is completed, they are
shipped overseas, become part of the RAF,
which keeps Nazis up nights. They are organ
ized in their own squadrons.
All of them — Czechs, Poles, French, Danes,
Dutch, Norwegians, Belgians, Slavs — are liv
ing, fighting proof that a free people can never
be conquered _ JERRY MASON
STUDENT,once, in a Belgian college.
Now he studies how to kill Nazis
PARIS was his home. lie eseaped,
lives now only to see it reborn
UNCONQUERED is this Hollander
who waits and works for revenge
ANONYMOUS: He got out of Norway,
and is helping to free it again

text:
Freedmen's Reports
10th Epidemic Death;
Six Babies Still III
The 10th Infant death due to!
diarrhea was reported at Freed
men’s Hospital today, pointing up
the need for a new and larger hos
pital which the Federal Security
Administration will seek in 1949.
One other baby was reported in!
a critical condition at the hospital,
which was swept by the epidemic
last month. Under the plan of re
leasing all babies as soon as pos
sible. only six remain at Freedmen's
Dow and all are ill.
Watson B Miller. Federal secur
ity administrator, said the epidemic
emphasized the need for an entirely;
new hospital.
"We have been working wdth the
Bureau of the Budget closely for
the past three years and have the
understanding that it will approve
funds for a new hospital after the
veterans' needs are relieved.”
Mr Miller said he hoped to ask
Congress for a hospital that would
cost ”2 or 3 million dollars" and
would be “the best of its kind in
the world."
Tomorrow afternoon Mr. Miller
will confer with Dr. James L. Hall,
superintendent of Freedmen's, and!
with Dr. Otis Anderson, head of the!
United States Public Health Serv-!
ire s hospital division with the view
to preventing recurrence of the dis
ease
Thus far physicians have been un
able to isolate an organism or virus
responsible for the epidemic Mr.
Miller said it was doubtful whether
the responsibility ever would be
placed.

PART 7.
MENACE OF CHEAP LABOR
World Economic Conference Is Taking Up the Problem of Poverty, Although This Work Is
L.ittle Discussed—Our Case Is Thoroughly American, Hozvever, and Does Not Go Into
the International Tangle—Natural Increase in Labor Supply Decreases Wages.
Drawn for The 8un4»r at*r MacMlne by J. Scott William*
A CONCERTED attack on the pov
erty problem is one of the less
talked about purposes of the
World Economic Conference. It
is by far the most important
of all economic problems. It is the one
problem which justifies the study of eco
nomics—if the study of economics can
be justified. Just what contribution to
that problem can be made by a world
economic conference remains to be seen.
From our own point of view the prob
lem of eliminating poverty is a peculiarly
national, and in no sense an interna
tional one. From the standpoint of most
of the other countries represented at the
World Economic Conference it has an
international aspect. In so far as they
can dump their unemployed upon us
they will experience relief. That, how
ever. will deepen our problem and tend
to reduce us to their level.
BY THOMAS NIXON CARVER
Professor of Political Economy of Harvard University.
There is danger that our represent
atives at the World Economic Confer
ence will play into the hands of those
countries whose favorite remedy for un
employment and poverty has been to
send their unemployed and their poor
to us. None of our representatives has
shown any appreciation of the real situ
ation, neither, for that matter, has the
administration in Washington, nor any
one closely connected with it. On the
contrary, Samuel Dickstein, chairman of
the House Committee on Immigration
and Naturalization, has introduced 13
separate bills for loosening up our immi
gration laws. The President himself has
never shown any interest in restriction.
Another very popular leader of the
Democratic party has declared himself
in favor of opening our doors to all the
people of the earth without regard to
race or nationality. That would mean
unrestricted immigration of coolie labor
from India, China and Japan as well as
of cheap labor from Europe, Eastern
Asia, Africa and the islands of the sea.
That would mean a vast increase of our
unemployment and poverty. Eventually
it would mean a reproduction in this
country of the chronic and biting misery
of those unhappy countries. If his policy
were adopted, all the benign reforms
now incubating in Washington would
benefit other laborers than our own.
n EVOLUTIONISTS are in the habit of
^ attacking our economic system on the
ground that it makes the rich con
tinually richer and the poor continually
poorer. It may be so mismanaged as to
produce those undesirable results; but
it may also be so managed as to pro
duce the opposite results. No economic
system is foolproof. If it is managed In
the interest of the rich and against the
interests of the poor, one set of results
will follow. If it is managed in the in
terests of the poor a different set of
results will follow. There is the pos
sibility that the rich, knowing exactly
what they want and how to get it, will

text:
Guardians of the Law
©Publishers' Photo Service
IN ZULULAND. There’s no snappier dresser among
the cops of the world than the native Zulu policeman of
South Africa. Note the hat, the sporty leggings and other
details, including bare feet.
HAITI’S POLICE ARE LABELED. If all other signs
fail in identifying these two as officers of the law, the hats
verify it. They patrol the streets of Port au Prince.
This girl, being of cast-iron determi
nation, was bound she’d get on in
the world. She poisoned her hus
band and his family to collect the
insurance. “Judge,” she said in
court, “they were all so old and
feeble, I couldn’t bear to see them
drag around any more." Where- "
upon the whole courtroom shed
tears of sympathy and she was
acquitted.
The combative girl Deep
thinker and all that, hut
disagrees strongly with the
press and public opinion.
Champions murders, un
. popular governments, and —
will uphold any cause that
hasn't a leg to stand on.

Left: IN THE CLASSIC STYLE. Powder
puff toes, tasseled hat and kilts set the po
lice of Athens, Greece, grandiosely apart
from any in the world. Here’s a venerable
member of the force.
THE EGYPTIAN “MOUNTED.” He
also gets his man, this Sudanese police
man on his lumbering camel. For desert
patrol his mount is far sturdier and more
dependable than a horse would be.
These lovely girls have very strong |
feelings about love and matrimony.
Nothing but good substantial settle
ments out of court can swerve them j
from suing parents for alienation of
husbands' affection, or erring hus
hands for alimony and fees.

ural lung power to carry
the day. [_

You wouldn't think
that this little wife is
exceptionally strong
minded, but she is.
Keeps it to herself,
and gets her way with
out husband even sus
— pecting. Lets him
think he's deciding for
himself.

A frail young girl in the
throes of love will show
unexpected powers of de
termined effort. If the
lucky swain stays away,
she will call on him. If he
refuses to answer the bell,
she will ring the janitor
and force an entrance on
the plea that he may be ill.
TOPPED BY A COOLIE HAT. That’s
about the only touch of Oriental style in
the uniform of this Chinese policeman who
pounds the sidewalks of Shanghai.

title: The Indianapolis times. [volume]

text:
4
pioneer & JBcmflcral.
9AIN T PAUL.
Sslnt Pool, Friititjr, Jnnuary 30, 1893.
The Telegraph News.
General Burnside has been superseded
by General Joseph Hooker. It is presumed
that his retirement was a voluntary
act, as it is known that he has re
peatedly asked to be relieved from
the command. General Hooker is a good
soldier and posesses enough bravery and
dash, but his friends have not the most en
tire confidence in his caution and good
judgment. Generals Sumner and Frank
lin are also relieved from command, and
we have a rumor that the army is about
to be broken up, and a portion transport
ed to the West.
—The Iribune says an officer has been
arrested for denouncing the policy of the
Administration on the slavery question. If
all officers and soldiers were arrested for
that offence, we should soon have but the
skeleton of an army.
—A change of administrative policy in
many essential respects, is predicted, from
the fact that Secretary Stanton has had a
conference at the White House, with sev
eral prominent Senators and Representa
tives.
—lt is said that if the President refuses
to change his Cabinet at the dictation of
Congress, that a vote will be passed de
claring a want of confidence in the Cabi
net. The people last fall declared a want
of confidence in the present Congress.
—The news from Galveston is interest
ing. Our squadron destined to recapture
that place, had arrived, and at the outset
was made the victim of about as smart a
trick as we have heard of during the war.
A rebel steamer, supposed to be tbe Ala
bama, was found inside, aud was hailed, and
answered that it was her Majesty’s ship
Spitfire. While a boat was being lowered
to board her, she fired a broadside into the
steamer Hatteras and sunk her, and then
escaped, followed by our old tub, the Pow
hatan. Talk of Yankee tricks!—the
rebels beat us two to one, every time, at
that game.
—We publish the address of General
Hooker to the Army of the Potomac. It
has the ring of determination, and we
only hope that he may have the support of
the administration, as well as the hearty
co-operation of the army.
—There is great alarm among the rebels
of North Carolina, occasioned by the mag
nitude of our offensive operations.
Major General I). N. Couch, late in
command of the second corps, succeeds
Gen. Sumner in command of the right
grand division of the Army of the Poto
mac, and Major General W. F. Smith, of
the sixth corps, succeeds Gen. Franklin
in command of the left grand division.
Major General George E. Meade, com
mander of the fifth corps, will probably be
the successor of Gen. Hooker.
—"While the gunboat Calhoun was as
cending the Teache river in Louisiana, she
got aground,tand Commodore Buchanan,
who landed and went ahead, was shot by
rebels concealed in rifle-pits. The rebels
were charged by a Vermont regiment, and
thirty of them captured and the rest dis
persed. The Captain of the rebel gun
boat Tallen was killed and his vessel cap
tured.
It is announced that when Gen. Butler
returns to New Orleans, in command of
the Department of the Gulf* Gen. Banks
will be ordered to Texas.
—A bill has been introduced into the
Senate for the seizure of three lines of
railroad converging at Chicago, from the
East. Tliis would seem to indicate the
breaking up of the Potomac Army and the
transportation West of a large force.
—The rebel officers in Bragg's army
admit that they were badly whipped at
Murfreesboro, and that the slaughter among
them was awful. It is stated, we believe
for the first time, that the Federals captur
ed 3,500 prisoners aud eight guns. It is
reported that the rebels have been rein
forced. A rebel General "Wheeler seems to
have taken the place of Morgan in the
guerilla line of operations, and ha? been
made a Major General and placed in com
mand of all the rebel cavalry.
—A bill for the removal of the Sioux
Indians has passed the Senate.
—The President and Cabinet have been
engaged in discussing the gauge of the Pa
cific Railroad, and have decided that it
shall he five feet. This is “important, if
true.”
—A rebel dispatch states that an attack
was made on Fort Caswell, North Caroli
na on the loth.
—Ben. Wai>e has been re-elected Sen
ator from Ohio lor the term of w six years
from the fourth of March.
—Brig. Gen. Silas Casey has been made
a Major General for his gallantry at the
battle of Fair Oaks, on the Peninsula—
and for voting to convict Fitz John Porter.
Annual Report of the Adjutant Gen
eral—Official Meanness Intensified.
A few days ago the Adjutant General,
Mr. Malmeos, handed us his Annual Re
port, with the object, as we presume, that
it should be examined and noticed in The
Pioneer. That is a courtesy to which we
very willingly address ourself; and if our
comments shall take the nature of unfa
vorable criticism instead of compliment,
the reason of it is to be found in the char
acter of the Report, rather than in any
prejudice against its author.
The Report makes up a volume of near
ly six hundred pages. Three-quarters *of
the Report is composed of tables, the pub
lication of which cannot be accounted for
on any other hypothesis than that of fur
nishing a profitable job to the public
printer. The illegitimacy of this printing
can bo realized when we state that, if the
Adjutant General of New York had pursued
a similar plan of publication, his Report
would have comprised at least forty thou
sand pages, at a cost to that State of from
one hundred and fifty to two hundred
thousand dollars. The fact that Minnesota
is smaller and has less force in the service, is
no justification for the printing of unneces
sary matter ; because our resources are
limited in proportion to our numbers; and
the cost of this publication falls as oner
ously on our Treasury, as the two hundred
thousand would on the Treasury of New
York.
The first thing that the most casual
reader of the Report will notice, is, the
entire absence of the official reports of the
officers in command of our frontier forces
during the Sioux war. These reports
would furnish the most complete and satis
factory account of the most remarkable
series of events that have occurred in the
history of any frontier people on this con
tinent. But, instead of thus spreading out
before our people and the world the accu
rate and succinct history which these mil
itary reports would furnish, the Adjutant
General ignores them entirely, and pub
lishes in their stead his own version of our
frontier disturbances. And this version,
we undertake to say, has never been sur
passed in any account of tbe same length,
by equal demerits of inaccuracy, favorit
ism, and injustice. These blemishes are
so marked, that they can be accounted for
only on the assumption that the Report
was prepared under the eye of the Gov
ernor of the State ; who, in this, as in va
rious other instances, has falsified, and
procured the falsification of, documentary
facts and records, in order to mould his
own chronic preferences and prejudices
into the history of the State, in the place
of the plain truth of events.
We cannot, of course, go over the
whole Report of the Adjutant General, in
a single article, and expose the errors of
omission and commission with which it
abounds. But we will take a single case,
as a sample of the whole. Our people all
know from cotemporary publications, the
part borne in this Sioux war by Major Jo
seph R. Brown. They know that, next
to the distinguished services rendered by
General Sibley, who was in command, the
part played by Major Brown contributed
most to the reclamation of the white pris
oners captured by the Sioux, and to the
successful issue of the campaign.
That the gallant and meritorious con
duct displayed by Major Brown, during
this Sioux war, should have been publicly
recognized by a suitable appointment in
some of the Regiments recently organized,
was not to be expected from the Admin
istration of Gov. Ramsey. The Governor
sii|l remembers that Brown was a mem
ber of the Board of Canvassers who de
cided that Ramsey was not elected Gov
ernor at the first election under the State
Constitution: and Ramsey attributes to
Brown’s superior arithmetical ability his
failure on that occasion. That was a
crime never to be forgiven ; and conse
quently Brown had nothing to expect
from Ramsey’s Administration, in recog
nition of services, however important they
might have been to the success of the
campaign.
But, Major Brown had reason to expect,
that, in the official report of the campaign,
by the Adjutant General of the State, his
services would be recognized in connec
tion with those of other officers with whom
he was asssociated. Ilis expectation has
not been realized. Ramsey’s Adjutant
General, in reporting the battle of Birch
Coolie, although he sometimes mentions
the services of Captains Grant and An
derson, yet with an extent of meanness
without parallel, entirely ignores the name
of Major Brown ; although the official re
port of Col. Sibley shows that the Major
was in command, and although the same
report of the Adjutant General gives
unmistakable evidence that the Major’s
official report, as such commanding officer,
was used by such Adjutant General, and
formed the ground-work of his narrative.
The Adjutant General admits that
the battle of Birch Coolie was the most bloody
of auy in which our forces liave been engaged
during the war thus fiir; and for the forces
THE WEEKLY PIONEER AND DEMOCRAT.
engaged, U one of the most bloody known iq
Indian warfare, in which the savages failed to
obtain the entire mastery.
The Adjutant General might have ad
ded, that it bad an important bearing upon
the final result of tbe campaign, as it gave
the first oheck to the Indians in their at
tacks upon the troops in the field, —di-
verted from their purpose a large party
who were marching to attack Mankato
and St. Peter, when such attack must
have been successful, and have added a
new chapter to the horrors of the frontier
massacres, —and taught the Indians the
dhtiger of permitting our troops to get be
tween them and their families, and thus
stopped all contemplated raids upon our
settlements during the balance of the cam
paign. Yet, the name of the command
ing officer of this expedition is suppressed,
even at the expense of making the report
ridiculous.
In his narration of the Birch Goolie af
fair, the Adjutant General says:
On the Ist of September the entire command
marched to the river bank, when the mounted
company, with one team and eight of the fa
tigue party crossed the river under protection
of the infantry. After search and examination
made in the vicinity of the Agency, and becom
ing satisfied that no Indians were in the neigh
borhood, Capt. Grant was directed to remain
with his company, and twelve of the fatigue
party and sixteen teams, on the east side of the
river. He was instructed to proceed as far as
the Beaver River, burying such dead as could
be found on the way. and thence return to the
upper timber on the Birch Coolie and encamp.
Now, Capt. Grant was the senior Cap
tain of the expedition. Who, then, “di
rected” him as to the movements and du
ties of his command ? Who “instructed”
him “to proceed as far as the Beaver
River,” and then “return to the upper
timber on the Birch Coolie, and encamp?”
Who commanded the troops that “crossed
the river,” and “after search and examin
ation,” became “satisfied that no Indians
were in the neighborhood,” and then is
sued the necessary orders to Capt. Grant ?
Again the Adjutant General reports:
The two divisions of the detachment during
this day buried the mutilated bodies of fifty
four victims of savage barbarity. 111 the even
ing they were re-united and weut iuto encamp
ment near the Birch Coolie, and about three
miles from the Lower Agency.
Nothing further is said of the operations
of the force that “crossed the river.” The
thorough examination of the country to
and about Little Crow’s village by the
whole party, and the farther explorations
by Major Brown in person, to ascertain if
Indians were located in the vicinity of the
Red Wood River, are subjects officially re
ported, as we find from our files, but en
tirely ignored by tbe Adjutant General,
because they could not well be mentioned
without referring to Major Brown.
The Adjutant General in his report, fur
ther says:
The camp was made in the usual way, with
the wagons packed around the occupied ground,
and the team horses fastened to the wagons.
The horses belonging to the mounted men
were fastened to a strong picket rope between
the tents and tiie wagons around the south
half of the camp, &c.
In this the Adjutant General exposes
his ignorance. In a military camp the
baggage wagons are never “ [tacked around
the occupied ground and, during the
whole campaign, there is no instance of
such a disposition of the baggage train,
except in the encampments of the Birch
Coolie expedition. It is the “ usual way ”
in which traders and other prairie travel
lers protect their camps, aud was ordered
by Major Brown, and the placing ot the
wagons superintended by him, at the first
encampment. It furnishes protection
against assault, while it is a guaranty
against the worst of evils attending an In
dian attack—that of a stampede of horses.
Fancy a hundred horses frightened by the
shots and yells of Indians, becoming rest
less and ungovernable, aud the advantage
to an assaulting party can readily be im
agined. Major Brown, in his disposition
of the wagons and horses, —the team
horses firmly tied, and encircling the en
tire camp, and the cavalry horses firmly
fastened in the centre, —added to the
strength of the barricade, while he made
the camp secure from the danger of a
stampede. But this the Adjutant General
describes as a “ usual ” military camp, to
evade reference to Major Brown.
Again the Adjutant General says:
That the enemy with a greatly superior force
should be successful in almost entering the en
campment. protected only by a wagon in any
one part, before those inside were aware of
their presence, and when they were scattered
and asleep throughout the tents, and, yet failed
to follow up their advantage by carrying the
place by storm, or at least attempting to do so,
is almost inexplicable.
There are doubtless many other things
inexplicable to the Adjutant General,
when he edn make a point by an affecta
tion of ignorance. We can discover noth
ing strange in the point he makes. It is
now well known that the programme of the
Indians was to assault the camp, create a
stampede of the horses, and during the
panic caused by such attack and stam
pede, to rush in, and, with the tomahawk
and short guns, make short work of the
troops. So confident were they of success,
that they sent back to their camp at Yel
low Medicine for women tp come forward
and take charge of their horses, teams,
tents, <fec. x Their plan succeeded admi
rably until they were discovered by our
sentinels. But, when they found that,
neither by firing nor by whooping, a stam
pede could be effected, and discovered that
the wagons and horses were so arranged
around the camp that they could not
storm it without exposing themselves to
the fire of our troops while they were
climbing into camp, they concluded the
attempt would be too hazardous, and re
tired to the timber.
Miyor Brown —according to the state
ment made to us a few days after by an
intelligent participator in the affair, —was
on his feet at the first fire, and instantly
comprehended all the dangers of the posi
tion of his command. His first step was
to urge the guard and others, as they seiz
ed their arras, to return the fire of the In
dians with rapidity. “Boys, don’t let
them approach ! give the red devils hell!”
was his admonition, as he moved from one
part of the camp to auother, to assure
himself that the horses were safe, and a
stampede improbable. “For a short time,
perhaps a minute or two, the firing was
all on the side of the Indians”, writes the
Adjutant General. This was during tbe
attempt to stampede the horses, and con
fuse our forces ; but they tailed in both,
and the promptness and rapidity with
which their fire was returned, taught them
that prudence was the better part of valor,
and iuducod them to withdraw to the
woods.
Twice during the heavy firing from the
Indians, Major Brown was saved by borses,
and at one and the same time a man was
shot on each side of him, and a horse in
his front; yet he continued to watch the
operations of the men, and was engaged
in placing a portion of Captain Grant’s
company in greater safety, and where
their fire could be more effective, when he
was struck by a ball in the back part of
the neck, which touched the spine, and
brought him to the ground senseless. lie
was then removed to the hospital tent by
Lieuts. Swan and Patch, where lie re
mained a few minutes, until consciousness
was restored. As soon as he was himself,
he returned, and superintended the con
struction of rifle pits, which the withdrawal
of the Indians rendered practicable. Of
these pits the Adjutant General writes :
Shortly after sunrise, the troops coiuiiienced
the excavation of rifle-pits immediately inside
•of the line of dead horses. But a email sup
ply of intrenching tools were to he had, as
there were but one pick and three spades in the
camp. The men. however, worked with a will,
using bayonets, knives, and tin pans, in lieu of
better implements. The ground was hard and
difficult of excavation; hut, notwithstanding
this, and the necessity of retaining their per
sons in such positions as afforded the least pos
sible exposure to the view of the savages,
thereby greatly impairing the effectiveness of
their labor, they soon had constructed an
available line of pits, of an aggregate length of
two hundred feet.
Here, again, arc the services of Major
Brown ignored. These pits were the sug
gestion of the Major’s experience in Indian
warfare, and were constructed under his
immediate supervision; and, when com
pleted, made his position capable of re ist
ing the assault of a thousand Indians.
Major Brown was in the ride-pits from
the time they were commenced until the
command was relieved by Col. Sibley,
constantly urging the men. and watching
the operations of the Indians, who con
tinued to fire upon the camp, though with
out doing other damage than killing the
horses.
Can Adjutant General M.ylmros give
any reason for the violation of military
courtesy displayed in his narrative of the
battle of Birch Coolie ‘i Major Brown
was in command of the expedition, and
did all that could be done in carrying out
the objects for which it was sent forward,
lie had made a close examination during
the day of the country lately occupied by
the Indians, and had every reason to- be
lieve that they hau all left. lie was cor
rect at the time of his reconnoisances; as
there were no Indians then encamped be
tween Crow Village and Yellow Medicine.
The force that surpised him were bouud
to a ditferent section of the country. They
discovered his camp by accident, and de
termined to attack it the next morning.
The vigilance and precautions of Major
Brown prevented them from succeeding
in their designs, while his courage and
self-control enabled him to get his com
mand almost instantly in a position of de
fence ; and in a short time afterwards, the
savages being repulsed, were compelled
to withdraw to the cover of the timber.
The defence of his camp was complete,
although his men suffered severely,
during the first few minutes of the
attack. Colonel Sibley opeuly attributed
to the coolness, courage, and pre
cautions of Major Brown, the saving of
the camp from the assault of the Indians,
and the probable assassination of every in
dividual in it; and yet, in reporting the
history of the affair, the Adjutant General
does not once refer to Major Brown, or to
any act of his by which even his presence
could he inferred, and much less that he
was in command and directing all the
movements in this battle of Birch Coolie,
which the Adjutant General officially de
scribes as “ the most bloody of any in
which our forces have been engaged dur
ing the war thus far, and, for the forces
en £&g®dj one of the bloodiest known in
Indian warfare, in which the savages failed
to obtain the entire mastery.”
We submit, in closing this review, based
as it is upon the official records of the
Birch Coolie affair, that the narrative giv
en to the public by Adjutant General
Malmros, is designedty and maliciously
false; a most cruel requital to Major
Brown for valuable and gallant services,
and for sufferings incurred while rendering
them for the defence and protection of our
people; and that it is, and must always re
main, a blot upon the official records of
the State. His narrative shows that he is,
in any moral, manly sense, unfit for the
position which he holds ; and if Governor
Ramsey was not the instigator of the in
justice we have exposed, he owes it to
public decency, and to relieve himself
from an endorsement of the wrong com
mitted, to remove this man from the office
he has disgraced.
The CommiKsionor of Statistic*.
Senator Daily yesterday introduced a bill to
abolish the office of Commissioner of Statistics.
This is but an expression of the general feeling
throughout the State, that under our present
circumstances it is absurdly extravagant to pay
two thousand dollars per year for collecting and
publishing the statistics of Minnesota. This is
a luxury which many Western States far wealth
ier than ours, have felt unable to afford:—es
pecially so at a time like this when taxes arc so
high, with no prospect of diminution. * * *
We doubt if a dozen Wheelocks, eacli with a
handsome salary, could induce an emigration
hitherward.—N7. Paul Union, 21 th.
We believe there is but one opinion in
regard to the superlative nonsense of sup
porting a Commissioner of Statistics at a
cost of $2,500 per annum, when all the
useful statistics requi. ed under the existing
law can just as well be forwarded to the
Secretary of State, and be spread before
the people in his Annual Report, as is
done in older and richer States. The
fact that the present Commissioner has
found time during the past year to edit
a daily newspaper, is conclusive proof that
his office is a sinecure, and that the sta
tistics of the State can be collated in a
much cheaper maimer. As it is now,
Minnesota cut a ridiculous figure among
her sister States. The office of Superin
tendent of Public Instruction, or of Com
mon Schools, abolished, and the important
educational interests of the State left with
out a responsible head, while it keeps up
a Bureau of Statistics, which are of no
earthly use, because they arc entirely un
reliable.
The Bureau was created, as we all know,
to give WiieeijOck a comfortable berth at
the expense ot the State, and if it is con
tinued, it will be for no other reason nou\
We repeat the suggestion, that all the
statistics which are of any value for refer
ence, or which may be of any interest to
the public, can be collated in the office of
the Secretary of State, and published in
his Annual Report, and a saving to the
State effected of over two thousand dol
lars per annum.
The Progress* ol' federal t surpatioii.
The first step taken by the Federal Ad
ministration towards concentrating all the
powers of government in its hands, was
in suspending the writ of habeas carpus ;
thus placing the liberty of every citizen
and inhabitant of the country, of every
age, sex, and condition, at the mercy of the
President of the United States, and the
ministers and agents who act under his
authority.
The second step was, the maturing of a
scheme by which all the State banking
machinery was to he destroyed, and all
State securities made valueless: and the
forcing of all the financial operations of
the Government and people to be carried
on with sij£h currency, alone, as the Fed
eral Treasury might choose to issue.
The third step is indicated in our tele
graphic dispatches of this morning—bv
which it will bo seen that the Federal
Government is arrogating to itself the
entire control of the State Militia..
With the personal liberties of the whole
people subject to its uncontrolled will and
disposition; with the finances of the whole
country, State and Federal, under its man
agement ; with the army and navy of the
United States, subject to its undisputed
authority; it needs only the transfer of the
whole militia force of the several States to
the Federal Government, to rentier it the
most absolute and irresponsible Despotism
that now exists, or that has ever existed,
on the face of the globe.
Sioux 'Treaties. Abrogated.
By our special dispatches it will be seen
that the bill for the abrogation of treaties
with the Sioux Indians, has passed the
Senate. It contains a provision for the
payment of SIOO,OOO to the sufferers by
the raid.
Col. John 15. Sanborn.
Among the nominations sent to the Sen
ate by the President, was that of Col. John
B. Sanboen, of the Minnesota Fourth, lor
a Brigadier General. Col. Sanboen has
well earned promotion for his general
ability as an officer, and especially for dis
tinguished gallantry at luka and Corinth.
More Printing Swindle*—The Repub
lican State Printer* becoming M
Outrageous in their Charges that
the State Auditor feels bound to Ex
pose them.
We were astonished on looking over the
Annual Report of the State Auditor, to
find that the charges rendered to him by
the public printers, were of such a swind
ling character that he felt himself officially
hound to expose them to the Legislature
and the people, in order to guard against
a repetition of like frauds in the future.
The Auditor does not furnish the figures
showing the sum total of such swindling,
and in this ; e is clearly guilty of an offi
cial sin ot omission. lie does, however,
give samples ol the charges rendered, from
which it is certain that hundreds and per
haps thousands ot dollars have been dis
honestly taken from the State Treasury
during the past year.
To show the character of this swindling,
wo extract from pages i) and 10 of the
Auditor’s Report for 1862, the following
copies of bills rendered for the incidental
printing of the last regular Session.
200 yeas and nays, comp. 1,000 ems. .80 63
Paper, 50 sheets, 2c l 00
Press work, 1 token r,i)
Binding, $1 65 per 100 copies 3 30
Members of the Legislature all know
what “yeas and nays ” are. They are
simply the names of the members printed
on a slip of paper, perhaps four inches by
eight. The legitimate charge for printing
the 200 copies is comprised in the first
three items of the above account, and it
amounts to $2.23. The fourth item is
where the stealing comes in; and that
amounts to $3.30 —or nearly one and a-half
times the legitimate charge, extra. That
is stealing on a right royal, liberal scale
stealing at the rate of one hundred and
fifty per cent. This $3.30 of stealing is
put down in the bill as “binding.” Will
the “ Press Printing Company,” which
executed this incidental printing, tell us
and the Legislature how they went to work
to “bind’’these “yeas and nays”? Of
course, cannot, because no binding
was ever done. The charge was con
structive, or, in other words, it was steal
ing; and, when they took the money from
the State Treasury on such a pretext, they
robbed the State. That is the plain Eng
lish of the transaction.
Another bill which the Auditor cites as
an example, is as follows :
300 per dieuicertificates, comp. 3,C00
ems 80 <l3
Paper. 35 sheets 70
Press work, 2 token* 1 20
Binding 4 05
This is of precisely the same character
with the one before considered, oidv that
the proportion of stealing is greater —
being $2.53 of legitimate charge, and
$4. 05 of stealing. For, as in the former
case, no binding was, nor could have been,
done.
As we said before, the Auditor neglected
his duty in not making a full exhibit of
this swindling and thieving business, in
detail. But, we presume his office con
tains the accounts on which the swindlers
have drawn money from the Treasury, the
past year; and, if so, the amount can he
ascertained. It is due to the tax-paying
public that this should be done: and that
the swindlers should he forced to disgorge !
their ill-gotten money.
Meantime, this exposure should serve as
a warning to Republican members, not to
commit the printing into the same hands j
again. If there is an honest Republican i
printer in the State, lie ought to be found j
out, and, on grounds of public interest, j
and to save the public Treasury from such j
knavish transactions in the future, he
should .be elected public printer. We
doubt very much if Republican members
will be able to find such a character in j
their party ; hut, if they can, they will 1
be doing themselves credit, and the State !
a service, by giving him the State pat
ronage.
The “Dismissal” ol “Goodrich”
from tire Service.
We find the following statement in the
St. Paul Press, of the 24th instant:
Goodrich, like Porter, was dismissed from
the army of the United States; but, unlike
Porter—alas!—lie was dismissed without a trial.
Anxious, always, to vindicate the char
acter of our cotemporary for truth and
veracity, we append the following letter
from the Adjutant General’s Office, in
proof of the fact of such “ dismissal ”:
Adjutant General’s Office, )
Wasiungtox, Nov. 21, 1862 j
Sir: Your resignation, tendered the 19th Oc
tober, to take effect the Ist November instant
lias been accepted by tiie President of the
United States, to take effect the 21st instant.
I am, Sir. very respectfully,
Your obedient servant
(Signed,) JAMES B. FRY,
, „ „ Ass ’t- Adjutant General,
('apt. Earle S. Goodrich,
Aide-de-Camp,
St. Paul, Minnesota.

The dispatches of Col. Sirley to Gen.
I’oi’E. are highly interesting and impor
tant. He gives an account of finding a
camp of friendly Indians, which embraced
one hundred and fifty lodges, and the re
lease of about one hundred white captives
and something over that number of half
breeds, and of a commission instituted by
him for the trial ot the Indians whom lie
has reason to believe are implicated in
murders and other outrages. He also
transmits a complete list of the white cap
tives released by the Indians.
In a dispatch to the Governor, Col. Sir
ley says:
Providentially, the captives in the hands of
the Indians have all been recovered, and are now
in my camp, with the exception of a few young
girls, who have been retained as the wives of
the young men. and a small number of boys, old
enough to drive the teams of the hostile party,
who have been taken with them. From the
bod information I can obtain, they do not num
ber more than twelve or fifteen, perhaps less.
1 send the captives to Fort Ridgley, with as
many comforts as we can furnish them.
Had Col. Sibley done nothing more
than to effect the release of these captives,
lie would have earned the applause of the
whole State. Ihe lives of these poor wo
men and children are worth more than the
souls of all the Indians in existence.
’lore Prisoners Escaped.
Mr. John F. Meagher, of Mankato, ar
rived here on the 2d. with Mrs. Prescott,
widow of Philander Prescott, interpreter,
who was killed at the Lower Agency, and
her daughter. Mrs. Prescott, with other
prisoners, was at the Indian camp, near
the mouth of the Chippewa river, when
the party left that place to attack Colonel
Sibley. After the war party left, an In
dian named White Dog took Mrs. Pres
cott’ and her daughter, and five persons of
Mr. Quinn's family, and started them
down the Minnesota river in a canoe. They
arrived at Fort liidgley last Saturday
night.
Mrs. Pre-wott gives a dreadful account
of the treatment of white captive women
by the Indians. They are subjected to
every indignity and outrage which can be
conceived. George Spencer is still alive,
and hopes are entertained that lie will yet
succeed in escaping.
From (lie Cltippcwa Country.
We learn from Rev. Mr. Peake that
Messrs. Rcffy, Rutherford, Henry Beau
leait and George Bunco returned from the
Grand Forks of the Red River to the Chip
pewa Agency and Crow Wing, on Friday
last, by way ot Red Lake, Leech Lake and
Gull Lake.
They report about 2,000 Chippewas to
have been collected at the Grand Forks, in
accordance with the call of the Commis
sioner of Indian Allairs, tor the purpose of
making a treaty with the Government.
Finding that no treaty was to be made,
they demanded, of Mr. Kittson, the Hud
son Bay Company’s goods, which he was
transporting through their country, and
look possession ot them, leaving him the
teams and empty wagons.
The Chief of the Red Dike Chippewas
told them they had been invited to join in
the late hostile demonstrations of llole-ix
tiie-Day; but he had instructed them not
to do so.
They agreed with the Pembina Indians
to make no treaty the coming winter, and
promised not to go to Washington.
It is to be hoped that the Government
may soon succeed in making a general
treaty with all the Chippewas.
Hattie of luka.
On our second page will be found a let

Camp Release (Opposite Mouth of\
Ciiutlwa River), Sept. 27, 1862. j
General : * * * Yesterday I came
to this place with my command, having
been met by several half-breeds with a flag
of truce. I encamped within five hundred
yards ot a large camp of about one hundred
and fifty lodges of friendly Indians and half
breeds, who had separated themselves from
Little Crow and tlie miserable crew with
him, and had secured from them most of
the white captives, awaiting my arrival.
About two o'clock in the afternoon I
paid a formal visit to the camp, attended
by the members of my staff and the com
manding officers of corps, with two com
panies of infantry as an escort.
Leaving the latter on the outside of the
line of lodges, I entered the camp, where I
found that regular rifle-pits had been con
structed, in anticipation of an attack by the
hostile Indians. I told the interpreter to
call the chiefs and head men together, for
I had something to say to them. The In
dians and half-breeds assembled accord
ingly in considerable numbers, and I pro
ceeded to give them briefly my views of
the late proceedings, my determination that
the guilty parties should he pursued and
overtaken, if possible, and 1 made a de
mand that all the captives should he deliv
ered to me instantly, that I might take
them to my camp.
After speeches in which they seriously
condemned the war party, and denied
any participation in their proceedings, and
gave me assurance that they would not
have dared to come and take my hand
their own was stained with the Mood of
the whites, they assembled the captive
women and children, and formally deliver
ed them up to me to the number of nine
ty-one pure whites. When taking the
names of such as had been instrumental
in obtaining the release of the prisoners
from the hostile Indians, and telling the
principal men I would hold another coun
cil with them [to-day, I conducted the
poor captives to my camp, where I had
prepared tents for their accommodation.
There were some instances of stolidity
among them, but, for the most part, the
poor creatures, relieved of the horrible
suspense in which they had been kept,
apd some of the younger women, freed
from the loathsome attentions to which
t hey had been subjected by their brutal
captors, were fairly overwhelmed with
jo.v*
I am doing the best I can for them, and
will send them down to-day, together with
a large number of half-breeds, who have
been also kept in restraint here. The first
mentioned are pure white women and
children, two or three of the latter being
very small orphans, all their relations hav
ing been killed. A list of them will ac
company this communication.
After the disastrous result to himself and
the bands associated with him, of the bat
tle of Wood Lake, the half-breeds report
that falling back to this point, they hastily
struck their tents and commenced retreat
ing in great terror. * * * *
I have issued an order appointing a
military commission, consisting of two
field officers and the senior Captain of tlie
Sixth Regiment (Col. Crooks, Lieut. Col.
Marshall, and Capt. Grant), for the exam
ination of all the men half-breeds, as well
as Indians in the camp near us, with in
structions to sift the antecedents of each,
so that if there are guilty parties among
thern, they can be arrested and properly
dealt with".
I have no doubt we shall find some such
in the number. 1 will report tlie result in
due time. I have a wounded prisoner in
my camp.
The number of half-'ireeds who were.re
tained by the hostile Indians as prisoners,
and now under my protection will consid
erably exceed one hundred, but the exact
number cannot now : -c 'liw u.
f am, (iviioral. very n.spceilully.
Your obedient servant,
H. It SIBLEY

A t; I'aiui Advance Ordered.
The Philadelphia Prests makes use of
the following significant language:
The armies of the Republic an* marshaling
for the i time in the history of the great re
bellion. The organization which exists now
must, remain to the end ot the war. Soon tin
advance wilt be made very generally, in every
direction and by every corps in the army. Tlie
day has been fixed—tlie leaders have been cho
i ana assigned to their positions, and the
lieeC'sarv orders have been written.
Emancipation a War measure.
The Republican, the radical organ at
Washington, has the following with refer
ence to the President’s proclamation:
We are to-day no nearer tlie possession of
Virginia than we were in April, 1861, and it is
doubtful whether the military strength of their
resistance to us lias not increased in quite as
great a proportion as our own. That is the
plain truth of the case, and we may as well look
it in the face.
It would seem that the President has looked
it in tlie face, and has ho'-ome satisfied that the
repression of this rebellion bn merely military meas
ures is an impossibility. That mas evident to
some f rom the start , ami to inereasiny numbers as
emits hare progressed.
If tlie Republican speaks in this the
opinions of the President, the confession
that the repression of this rebellion by
merely military measures is impossible, wil
make food for reflection for the more
thoughtful minds.
The New York Independent, which
heartily endorses the President’s Emanci
pation Proclamation, nevertheless expresses
regret that the decree of emancipation is
not immediate and unconditional, and, in
stead of being based on considerations of
expediency, founded on the principle of
justice to the slave. Tlie desires of the
Independent, in these respects, would be
reasonable, if the proclamation had any
reference to philanthropic considerations.
But we understand it to be exclusively a
war measure—issued for the same reasons
that an order to burn a town, or to do any
other act directed against the enemy, would
be issued—in which light, alone it is to be
considered, and in which respect it calls for
the support of the people, as any other
order of the Commander-in-Chief calls for
such support.
Were its primary purpose tlie emanci
pation of the blacks it would be peculiarly
censurable in the direction indicated by
the Independent, tor it contemplates no
conditions of humanity nor principles of
justice so far as the slaves themselves are
concerned. Their freedom is made by it
dependent on no merits of their own, but
contingencies connected with others. It is
no justice to them but punishment to oth
ers that dictates it.
There is no occasion for getting confused
over the matter. All the great ends of
freedom may flow from it, as contemplated
by its enthusiastic admirers, and when
they do, it will be ample time to rejoice
accordingly. At present it has no other
aspect, as it has no other purpose, than as
a war measure— a means of hastening the
termination of the rebellion—and in that
light only is it profitable now to consider
it. As a war measure, there will he dif
ferences of opinion as to its value, though
none as to the propriety of supporting the
President in making it as effective as pos
sible.
How JleC’lellan will .’love In Vir
ginia.
It is said that military authorities in
Washington concur that Gen. McClellan
will not push the bulk of his great army
into the valley of the Shenandoah, but,
that he will leave a sufficient force in that
quarter, and making Washington his base
of operations, advance into Virginia to
wards the Rappahannock. The Virginia
Central Railway is the true base of opera
tions of the enemy, and one in which they
hold great advantages over ns, no matter
how our forces may he disposed. Extend
ing from Richmond to Staunton, if enables
them to rapidly concentrate their army
against our advance north of tlie Pamunkv
river, and at the same time is tapped at
each end hv other railroads, which aflbrd
the best facilities for furnishing supplies
and reinforcements. Therefore, this ar
tery of the rebellion in Virgiuia, must be
severed before we succeed in regaining
that State.

The Situation in Kentucky.
We are not fully advised of the position
and movements of our armies in Kentucky,
and would not be at liberty to give infor
mation to that effect if it were in our pos
session. Of one fact, however, we are j
confident: There are now troops enough
in Kentucky, and in the camps of the 1
Northwestern Slave-', to move upon and
overwhelm the rebels in Kentucky without
delay, and it is of vital importance to move
at once. < Mir new troops may be “ raw ”
and the veterans weary, but we are con
vinced that now is the time to move. It is
an intolerable mortification to see the rebels
running riot over the State of Kentucky,
and her best citizens driven into exile or
subjected to persecutions at home. All
considerations of national honor and safety
are against the continuance of this dis
tressing and most disastrous state of
affairs.
It is not doubted that every day of delay
in Kentucky adds immensely to the stores
of rebel supplies for the ensuing winter.
It is indicated that the main object of the
invasion of this State is to sweep olf the
whole stock of provisions the State aflbrds,
and it is hardly thought probable that, ;
even if let alone by our armies, they would .
attempt to hold the whole State through j
tiie winter.

Col. Sibley’s Dispatcher .
The dispatches of 001. Sibley to Gen.
Pope, are highly interesting and impor
tant. He gives an account of finding a
camp of friendly Indians, which embraced
one hundred and fifty lodges, and the re
lease of about one hundred white captives
and something over that number of half
breeds, and of a commission instituted by
him for the trial of the Indians whom he
has reason to believe are implicated in
murders and other outrages. He also
transmits a complete list of the white cap
tives released by the Indians.
In a dispatch to the Governor, Col. Sib
ley says:
Providentially, the captives in the hands of
the Indians have all been recovered, and are now
in my calnp, with the exception of a few young
girls, who have been retained as the wives of
the young men, Mid a small number of boys, old
enough to drive the teams of the hostile party,
who have been taken with thenj. From the
best information I can obtain, they do not num
ber more than twelve or fifteen, perhaps less.
I send the captives to Fort Ridgley, witli as
many comforts as we can furnish them.
Had Col. Sibley done Nothing more
than to effect the release of these captives,
he would have earned the applause of the
whole State. The lives of these poor wo
men and children are worth more than the
souls of all the Indians in existence. '
More Prisoner* Escaped.
Mr. John F. Meagher, of Mankato, ar
rived here on the 2d, with Mrs, Prescott,
widow of Philander Prescott, interpreter,
who was killed at the Lower Agency, and
her daughter. Mrs. Prescott, with other
prisoners, was at the Indian camp, near
the mouth of the Chippewa river, when
the party left that place to attack Colonel
Sibley. After the war party left, an In
dian named White Dog took Mrs. Pres
cott and her daughter, and five persons of
Mr. Quinn’s family, and started them
down the Minnesota river in a canoe. They
arrived at Fort Ridgley last Saturday
night.
Mrs. Prescott gives a dreadful account
of the treatment of white captive women
by the Indians. They are subjected to
every indignity and outrage which can be
conceived. George Spencer is still alive,
and hopes are entertained that he will yet
succeed in escaping.
From the Chippewa Country.
We learn from Rev. Mr. Peake that
Messrs. Ruffy, Rutherford, Henry Beau
leau and George Bungo returned from the
Grand Forks of the Red River to the Chip
pewa Agency and Crow Wing, on Friday
last, by way of Red Lake, Leech Kike and
Gull Lake.
They report about 2,000 Chippewas to
have been collected at the Grand Forks, in
accordance with the call of the Commis
sioner of Indian Affaire, tor the purpose of
making a treaty with the Government.
Finding that no treaty was to be made,
they demanded, of Mr. Kittson, the Hud
son Bay Company’s goods, which he Mas
transporting through their country, and
took possession ot them, leaving him the
teams and empty wagons.
The Chief of the Red Lake Chippewas
told them they had been invited to join in
the late hostile demonstrations of Hole-in
the-Day ; but he had instructed them not
to do so.
They agreed with the Pembina Indians
to make no treaty the coming winter, and
promised not to go to Washington.
It is to be hoped that the Government
may soon succeed in making a general
treaty with all the Chippewas.
Battle of luka.
On our second page will be found a let
ter from an intelligent correspondent, giv
ing an account of the battle of luka, in-,
which Gen. Robecrans defeated the rebel
army under Gen. Price. That the criti
cisms on the management of the alfair. are
just, there can be no doubt. The corres- j
pondent of the Cincinnati Commercial i
says: ‘‘Drunkenness in high places pre- j
vented us from capturing General Price, j
with his twenty-three thousand men, which
could easily have been done.” Drunken- ;
ness, under such circumstances, is scarcely j
less infamous than treason.
A Confewilon.
It has been stated, we think, that the {
Indian outbreak and massacre of women j
and children in Minnesota was the indirect !
if not the positive work of the rebel lead- j
ers. Monstrous as the assertion is, it is j
true by their own Confession. The proof
is in the following paragraph, which we
cut from the Richmond Dispatch , of the
24th instant: V
As we Kjtracjjrfco.—'She Yankees are about
to send their army capped at Haiyer’s Ferry
agninsttlre Indians. Has the Govommenf no
means of retaliation for such a Preach of faith ?
THE INDIAN WAR.
IMPORTANT DISPATCHES.
Ole Hundred and Fifty Ledges
of Friendly Indians.
One Hundred White Cap
tives Released.
The Captives Overwhelmed
with Joy.
ONE HUNDRED HALF-BREEDS
RELEASED.
COL. SIBLEY WILL HANG THE GUILTY.
LITTLE (ROW AT BIG STONE

STANDING BUFFALO’S BAND AGAINST HIM.

DISPATCHES FROM COL. SIBLEY TO GEN. POPE.
Camp Release (Opposite Mouth of)
Chippewa River), Sept. 27,1862. J
Genebal : * * * Yesterday I came
to this place witli my command, having
been met by several half-breeds with a flag
of truce. I encamped within five hundred
yards of a large camp of about one hundred
and fifty lodges of friendly Indians and half
breeds, who had separated themselves from
Little Crow and the miserable crew with
him, and had secured from them most of
the white captives, awaiting my arrival.
About two o’clock in the afternoon I
paid a formal visit to the camp, attended
by the members of my 6taff and the com
manding officers of corps, with two com
panies of infantry as an escort.
Leaving the latter on the outside of the
line of lodges, I entered the camp, where I
found that regular rifle-pits had been con
structed, in anticipation of an attack by the
hostile Indians. I told the interpreter to
call the chiefs and head men together, for
I had something to say to them. The In
dians and half-breeds assembled accord
ingly in considerable numbers, and I pro
ceeded to give them briefly my views of
the late proceedings, my determination that
the guilty parties should be pursued and
overtaken, if possible, and I made a de
mand that all the captives should be deliv
ered to me instantly, that I might take
them to my camp.
After speeches in which they seriously
condemned the war party, and denied
any participation in their proceedings, and
gave me assurance that they would not
have dared to come and take my hand if
their own was stained with the blood of
the whites, they assembled the captive
women and children, and formally deliver
ed them up to me to the number of niue
ty-one pure whites. When taking the
names of such as had been instrumental
in obtaining the release of the prisoners
from the hostile Indians, and telling the
principal men I would hold another coun
cil with them [to-day, I conducted the
poor captives to my camp, where I had
prepared tents for their accommodation.
There were some instances of stolidity
among them, but, for the most part, the
poor creatures, relieved of the horrible
suspense in which they had been kept,
and some of the younger women, freed
from the loathsome attentions to which
they had been subjected by their brutal
captors, were fairly overwhelmed with
joy.
I am doing the best I can for them, and
will send them down to-day, together with
a large number of half-breeds, who have
been also kept in restraint here. The first
mentioned are pure white women and
children, two or three of the latter being
very small orphans, all their relations hay
ing been killed. A list of them will ac
company this communication.
After the disastrous result to himself and
the bands associated with him, of the bat
tle of Wood Lake, the half- breeds report
that falling back to this point, they hastily
struck their tents and commenced retreat
ing in great terror. * * * *
I have issued an order appointing a
military commission, consisting of two
field officers and the senior Captain of the
Sixth Regiment (Col. Crooks, Lieut. Col.
Marshall, and Capt. Grant), for the exam
ination of all the men half-breeds, as well
as Indians in the camp near us, with in
structions to sift the antecedents of each,
so that if there are guilty parties among
them, they can be arrested and properly
dealt witlju
I have no dou\»t we shall find some such
in the number. I will report the result in
due time. I have a wounded prisoner in
my camp.
The number of half-breeds who were,re
tained by the lwstile Indians as prisoners,
and now under my protection will consid
| erably exceed one hundred, but the exact
i number cannot now be given,
i I am. General, very respectfully,
Your obedient servant,
; . H. H. SIBLEY,
/ \ ‘ • Colonel Commanding.
Camp Kklkask, xeam Lao Qci Paiiok, Li
September 28,1882. )
: L have the honor to re
' ■Vto my dispatch of yesterday for a,
THE WEEKLY PIONEER AND DEMOCRAT
. ", 1 "i'* ■» ■" ***' l
detail of my military operations in this
quarter, f fcfe apprehended sixteen In
dians in the friendly camp adjoining, who
are suspected of being participators in the
late outrages, and I have appointed a mili
tary commission of five officers to try
them. I enclose a copy of the order di
recting it. If found guilty, they will be
immediately executed, although I am
somewhat in dobbt whether my authority
extends quite so far. An example Is, how
ever, imperatively necessary, and I trust
you will approve the act, should it happen
that some real criminals have been seized
and promptly disposed of.
I have information, apparently reliable,
that Little Crow and his adherents are at
Big Stone Lake, sixty-five miles above this,
where it is supposed he will be stopped by
Standing Buffalo’s Sisseton band of Sioux,
as I have had a correspondence with that
chief, who desires to remain on friendly
terms with our government.
Very respectfully,
Your obedient servant,
H. H. SIBLEY,
Colonel Commanding.

Ninety-one whites, and the rest half

Number of Slaves to be Emancipated.
Should the rebellion continue in its pres
ent shape until the Ist of January next,
the number of slaves which will on that
day be virtually emancipated, under the
proclamation of the President, will be as
follows:
Alabama 435,132
Arkansas 111,104
Florida 61,753
Georgia 462,232
Louisiana 333,010
Mississippi 436,696
North Carolina 331,081
South Carolina. 402,541
Tennessee 275,784
Texas 180,682
Eastern Virginia 375,000
Total, according to census of 1860. .3,405,015
The natural increase will probably make
the aggregate at the present time about
3,500,000.
A Grand Advanee Ordered.
The Philadelphia Presto makes use, of
the following significant language:
The armies of the Republic are marshaling
for the last time in the history- of the great re
bellion. Tlie organization which exists now
must remain t<o the end of the war. Boon an
advance will be made very generally, in every
direction mid by every corps in the army. The
day- has been fixed—the leaders have been cho
seu and assigned to their positions, and 'the
, necessary orders have bbon rirfitten. ; .
ON
The Republican, the radical organ at
Washington, has the following irfth refer
ence to the President’s proclamation -.
We are today no nearer the possession of
Virginia than we were in April, 1861. and it is
doubtfhl whether the military strength of their
resistance to ns has not increased in quite as
great a proportion as oar own. That is the
I)lain truth of the ease, and we may as well look
t in the foce.
It would im«i that the President has looked
it in the face, cmd hat borome satisfied that the
repression of this rebellion by merely military meas
ures is an impossibility. That was evident to
some from the start, and to increasing numbers as
events have progressed.
If the Republican speaks in this the
opinions of the President, the confession
that the repression of this rebellion by
merely military measures is impossible, wil
make food for reflection for the more
thoughtful minds.
The New York Independent, which
heartily endorses the President’s Emanci
pation Proclamation, nevertheless expresses
regret that the decree of emancipation is
not immediate and unconditional, and, in
stead of being based on considerations of
expediency, founded on the principle of
justice to the slave. The desires of the
Independent, in these respects, would be
reasonable, if the proclamation had any
reference to philanthropic considerations.
But we understand it to be exclusively a
war measure—issued for the same reasons
that an order to burn a town, or to do any
other act directed against the enemy, would
be issued—in which light alone it is to be
considered, and in which respect it calls for
the support of the people, as any other
order of the Commander-in-Chief calls for
such support.
Were its primary purpose the emanci
pation of the blacks it would be peculiarly
censurable in the direction indicated by
the Independent, for it contemplates no
conditions of humanity nor principles of
justice so far as the slaves themselves are
concerned. Their freedom is made by it
dependent on no merits of their own, but
contingencies connected with others. It is
no justice to them but punishment to oth
ers that dictates it.
There is no occasion for getting confused
over the matter. All the great ends of
freedom may flow from it, as contemplated
by its enthusiastic admirers, and when
they do, it will be ample time to rejoice
accordingly. At present it has no other
aspect, as it has no other purpose, than as
a war measure— a means of hastening the
termination of the rebellion—and in that
light only is it profitable now to consider
it. As a war measure, there will be dif
ferences of opinion as to its value, though
none as to the propriety of supporting the
President in making it as effective as pos
sible.
How 9lcCTellan will Move 111 Vir
ginia.
It is said that military authorities in
Wasliiugton concur that Gen. McClellan
will not push the bulk of his great army
into the valley of the Shenandoah, but,
that he will leave a sufficient force in that
quarter, and making Washington his base
of operations, advance into Virginia to
wards the Rappahannock. The Virginia
Central Railway is the true base of opera
tions of the enemy, and one in which they
hold great advantages over us, no matter
how otir forces may be disposed. Extend
ing from Richmond to Staunton, it enables
them to rapidly concentrate their army
against our advance north of the Pamunky
river, and at the same time is tapped at
each end by other railroads, which afford
the best facilities for furnishing supplies
and reinforcements. Therefore, this ar
tery of the rebellion in Virginia, must be
severed before we succeed in regaining
that State.
A Little Too Mncli.
Ever since the arrival home of Cassius
M. Clay, the telegraph has been burthen
ed with the disposition that was to be made
of him by the Government. It was stated
that he was going to New Orleans, and
then that he was to have a command in
Kentucky, and then that he would return
to Russia, etc. We believe in Cassius the
Government has got an elephant, and don’t
know what to do with him. Here is the
latest dispatch about him, which we find
in the Chicago Tribune:
It is again stated that C. M. Clay is about to
return to Russia. His views as to military man
agement are said to be not such as in the opin
ion of the government would warrant it in giv
ing him a military command commensurate
vrith his just claims. As Cameron desires to
return home it is thought liest now to send him
back to Russia again. It is understood that
Cameron again wishes to return-with a view to
canvassing the field for senatorial successes in
Pennsylvania.
We pray that he may not be sent abroad
to again disgrace our nation in tjhe eyes of
all Europe. Let him be sent up to Min
nesota to fight Indians—We can stand the
infliction —if the Indiana can. ; > j

luka, Miss., Sept. 22, 1862.
To the Editor of the Pioneer.
I seize a moment after the very confused
and wearisomb labors of a day in a great
and unorganized hospital, to state as defin
itely as I am able our losses in the recent
short but very severe encounter with Price’s
heavy force near this place, particulars of
which I have no time to give, but only
casualties.
KILLED.
Benjamin Poole, Co. C.
J. O. Russell, Co. F,
Geo. K. Campbell, Co. G.
James Casey, Co. C.
Jones, Co. 11.
Thomas Smith, Co. F.
WOUNDED.
John Boss, Co. E, leg.
Aaron B. Moore, Co. K, I jack and breast.
S. L. Mannery, Co. D. leg.
B. Versmout, Co. G, arm.
Thomas Reeves, Co. C, two fingers off.
Anton Montrell, Co. G, head.
Tour Oleson, Co. A, arm and leg.
JoJmEiek, Co. G, leg.
Geo. Hutchinson, Co. A, through breast.
John E. McCann, Co. K, hand and thigh.
George W. Thomas, Co. E, leg.
J. B. Perkins, Co. D, leg by bayonet.
Orderely Sergt. J. W. Dunn, Co. B, leg.
Orderly Sergt. G. G. Kimball, Co. D, back.
Lieut. J. A. Goodwin, Co. E, thigh.
J. E. Sampson, Co. E, back.
Edward Siebarth, Co. B, thiglk
Chas, Olson, Co. H, both legs.
Geo. Winchell, Co. F, shoulder.
S. M. Milhollin, Co. K, back.
John Fobbe, Co. G, ancle.
Sergt. Samuel T. Isaacs, Co. I, hand.
A. 11. Kellogg. Co. D, ancle.
Geo. Reeder, Co. G, both ancles.
C. G. Mickle, Co. B, shoulder.
Enos A. Bunker, Co. E, leg.
Geo. A. Clark, Co. D, thigh.
Isaac De Toteil, Co. K. arm and leg.
Orlaudo Lindersmith, Co. E, leg.
Corporal J. Burdick. Co. F, head, slightly, i
Sergt; Phelps, Co. E, groin.
Patrick Loltus, Co. G, hand.
N. A. Abell, Co. B, missing.
Cameron, Co. E, missing.
Capt. Lueg, Co. G, was injured by a horse
which fell and lay upon him for twenty min
utes.
The three regiments at the right of us
suffered more severely than we. The
brunt of theffight was born by our brig
ade. The charge of the rebels was sud
den and desperate. Our brigade mustered
hardly beyond two thousand men. In a
half hour’s fight one hundred and fifty
were killed and four hundred wounded.
The rebels engaged in whole or in part
four brigades. They lost somewhat more
heavily than we. The commanders of the
Third Texas and Third Louisiana and
seven of the captains of these two regi
ments, are wounded and in our hands.
We took the colors of the Third Texas.
Col. Eddy, of the Fourty-eighth Indiana, is
wounded in the arm severely, and Colonel j
Boomer, of the Twenty-fifth Missouri, I fear j
fatally shot through the breast. Col. San- I
born commanded the brigade and behaved
gallantly. The Fourth did its work, as it
always will, manfully. Capt. Legro com
manded. The regiment is now, I learn,
at luka, and rumor has it is about to set |
off Ripley-wise after the fleet footed Price, j
Of the circumstances of the fight you will !
have learned ere this reaches you. So I I
leave it all: May God be merciful and ;
comfort those who through this letter learn
of their bereavements.
Yours, respectfully, A. S. FISKE.
Chaplain Fourth Minnesota Vols.
The Situation In Kentucky.
We are not fully advised of the position j
and movements of our armies in Kentucky, j
and would not be at liberty to give infer- j
mation to that effect if it were in our pos
session. Of one fact, however, we are
confident: There are now troops enough
in Kentucky, and in the camps of the
Northwestern States, to move upon and
overwhelm the rebels in Kentucky without
delay, and it is of vital importance to move
at once. Our new troops may be “ raw ”
and the veterans weary, but we are con- i
vinced that now is the time to move. It is !
an intolerable mortification to see the rebels ]
running riot over the State of Kentucky,
and her best citizens driven into exile or ■
subjected to persecutions at home. All
considerations of national honor and safety |
are against the continuance of this dis
tressing and most disastrous state of
affairs.
It is not doubted that every day of delay (
in Kentucky adds immensely to the stores
of rebel supplies for the ensuing winter.
It is indicated that the main object of the
invasion of this State is to sweep off tlie
whole stock of provisions the State affords,
and it is hardly thought probable that,
even if let alone by our armies, they would
attempt to hold the whole State through
the winter.
Remains of George Gleason Found.
We learn that 001. Sibley’s expedition
on its way to the Yellow Medicine, found
the remains of George Gleason, and had
them properly interred. They were re
cognized by papers found in his pockets.
—A letter to the Baltimore American
from the , : army of the Upper potomac,
Isays:
i A ride froiq the centre of tlie army of the
! Potomac to its right wiug at Wffiiamsport,
j gives one an expressive idea of the immense
; mufiber of men snd aggregation of material
that baa been brought together., Foe thirteen

gave Inmsdfurnto the pickets of the Sth.Mary
lantl. Heated lib was utterly tired of the
rebel sendee; and Would sooner he ia r Port Mo-

CASUALTIES AT IUKA.
TJ»e Scheme of Invading the North a
... Failure.
With the AxpulsjcH of L m and Jack
bon’z Rebels, from Maryland, by General
McClsllan, and the thwarting of the Wes
tern invaders by Weight, Nelson and
Buell, the mad scheme of invading the
loyal States, forced upon the traitor Con
gress by such inftiriated demagogues as
Pobcher Miles, has run its short and
troublous career to ignominious failure.
It no doubt had its political as well as mil- ;
itary design. Forced in part by the utter
beggary of the districts which the Rebel
armies had blighted by their presence, and
j which no longer furnished entertainment
| for either man or beast, the aggressive
; movement was doubtless also intended as
j a sham to deceive Europe, by passing off a
j spasm of feverish energy as a demonstra
; tion of enduring vigor and strength. But
I the disastrous failure of this effort must
| tell with damaging efi'ect against their
| cause. The astute statesman across the
Atlantic will hardly have had time to ex
amine and digest the record of the tempo
rary successes of the Rebels before being
overtaken by the intelligence of the prompt
and emphatic punishment they received;
of the instant uprising of Pennsylvania and
Ohio, of the powerful reinforcements sent
to the Union armies, and of the splendid
victories of South Mountain and Antietam,
by which the Rebels were sent howling j
across the Potomac.
Looking back from to-day, over the last 1
few weeks, we may well congratulate our- |
selves upon the utter failure and defeat of j
the Rebel plan of a Northern aggressive j
war, East and West, which had its politi- •
cal as well as its military significance, and j
which, after such prompt repulse in the
West, and terrible punishment in the East, j
will scarcely be counseled again, even by j
such hotheads as Miles and Foote.
Promotion* and Order*.
The following named officers have been i
promoted, on the recommendation of Major
General McClellan, to Brigadier Generals:
Colonel Governeur A. Warren, of the Fifth
New York Volunteers, for distinguished
conduct at the battle of Gaines’ Mill; Col
Alfred Sully, First Minnesota, for distin
guished conduct at the battle before Rich
mond ; Col. William N. Averill, Third j
Pennsylvania, for distinguished conduct at 1
Fair Oaks ; and Col. Robert Cowden, First j
Massachnsett-, for distinguished conduct at 1
Williamsburg.
Brigadier General John Cook, United
States Volunteers, has been ordered to re
port for duty to Major General Pope. • i
Surgeon William A. Sloan, U. S. A., has 1
been assigned to duty with Gen. Pope, as !
Medical Director of the Department of the
i Northwest.
