title: Evening star. [volume]

text:
THE SILVER STATE.
Friday.December 14. IKK?.
TDK COXSTITITIOX.iL COXVEXTIOX.
Nearly all the newspapers in the
State are opposed to calling a conven
tion to revise the State Constitution or
frame a new one. On the other hand, the
lawyers who have expressed an opinion
on the question, are nearly all in favor
of a convention. All the State officers
and members of the Legislature elect
are virtually pledged to use all honor
able means in their power to secure the
passage of au Act to revise the Consti
tution. This pledge, which was em
bodied in the platforms of both political
parties at the recent election, they
agreed to abide by without equivoca
tion, or, so far as the people were aware
of, mental reservation. How are the
papers which oppose the convention
going to release the State officers
and members of the Legislature from
their obligations ? The assertion,
"****•» piafcformg are merely traps to
catch voters,” or that “pledges given
by a candidate as a private citizen be
fore election are not binding on him as
an officer after election,” will not be
deemed sufficient by a conscientious
legislator to release him from his obli
gations. Party platforms ought to
mean what they say, and every mem
ber of the Legislature elected last No
▼ember, who means to abide by the
platform on which he was elected, has
no honorable alternative but to advo
cate and vote for the passage of an Act
providing for a Convention to revise
the Constitution. If this Convention
ia not the moat effective and cheapest
method of revising the Constitution
and changing it to meet the require
ments of the times, the fault is not his
bat that of the people who elected him
with the expressed understanding that
be should do so. If they (the people)
have changed their minds on the sub
ject, let them give evidence of the fact
by petitioning the Legislature to that
effect, and thus release the membes
from their obligations.
MEW EMBLASD TOBACCO CIKOWEB*.
A convention of tobacco men was
held in Hartford, Connecticnt, this
week. It was attended by about four
hundred persons who are engaged in
raising tobacco in Connecticut and Mas
sachusetts, who organized an associa
tion for mutual benefit ami
protection. It was stated in
the Convention that tobacco im
ported from Sumatra, an island in
the Iddian Ocean, was seriously injuring
the business of tobacco growers in New
England, and speeches were made fa
voring a tax of a dollar a pound on ci
gar wrappers imported from Sumatra.
In that island tobacco is cultivated by
coolie labor with which the white labor
of Yankeedom cannot compete, and
the Yankees at once organize
for protection against it, but when the
white laborers of the United States, who
could not compete with coolie labor,
asked for legislation to restrict it, the
men who now clamor for protection
against the products of that labor, de
nounced the Pacific Coast workingmen
as barbarians, and indorsed the action
of Hoar and others in Congress, who
opposed a bill to prevent coolie immi
gration. They pronounced labor organ
izations for protection against coolieism
on this coast communistic, but consider
it eminently proper to organize them
selves into a mutual protection associa
tion the moment they have to compete
with the product of coolie labor. New
England philanthropy embraces the
whole hmnan family, regardless of race
or color, provided the philanthropists
lose nothing by their generosity.
At a mooting of the Board of Aldsr
men in Boston, Joseph Caldwell, the
Citizens’ candidate for the next Board,
and a member at present of the city
government, made a motion, which was
adopted, that the votes intended, but
irregularly oaat for Bdwin F. Leigh
ton (Dem.), be credited to Leighton,
which elects him. If they had net
been counted Caldwell would be elec
ted.
A meeting of the pig iron manufac
turers of the United States is being
held at Pittsburg, for the purpose of
forming a protective association. Ac
tion will be taken in regard to a more
equitable tariff than that recommended
by the Commission.
TELEdKAFHIC ITEMS.
At Pioneer, Arizona, William Hardy
stepped to the bar to take a drink,
when Thomas Kerr, without any prov
ocation, knocked him down. Kerr
then drew his pistol, saying: "Young
fellow, now I’ve got you,” and placing
the pistol on his breast fired, killing
him instantly. Kerr was disarmed. A
jury of twelve men held an informal
trial. Kerr asked for an hour’s time to
arrange his business. He sat down,
and with perfect coolness wrote to his
mother at Lexington, Illinois, request
ing that all his effects be given to her.
He then asked for several drinks. The
citizens then took him out to a syca
more tree, where he made a few re
marks, confessing the killing of several
men. Th y drew him up once and let
him down. He then asked permission
to take off his boots, saying that he did
not want to die with his boots on. The
request was granted, and they then
8Wrung him up.
Colonel Babcock, a Republican Pro
hibitionist, is in Chicago. He said:
"I have spoken in the large towns and
cities of California, and have always
found large and interested audiences.
The most ultra Prohibition sentiment
has seemed to meet the most cordial
approval. The Prohibition Executive
Committee, of which I am a member,
__i _■ ■_
Prohibition clubs in every county, and
the party will maintain its organization
until the Republicans adopt some poli
cy that looks toward a stringent restric
tion upon liquor traffic and final prohi
bition.” Babcock thiuks that in lets
than three years the temperance ques
tion will be the leading political issue
in the country.
At San Beruardino, California, James
Clark was accidentally shot dead by his
brother in a fight with a stranger.
Clark drew his pistol, and his brother
took it from him, and commenced beat
ing the other man over the head with
it, when it was accidentally discharged,
the ball striking his brother in the
forehead, causing instant death. No
arrests.
The Washington Star, on the subject
of Reerdell turning State’s witnec3 in
the Star Route cases, says: It is
thought by those who have closely ob
served the proceedings that Reerdell
will take the steps indicated in case be
sees before the trial closes it will affoid
the only retreat for him to escape the
penitentiary.
Secretary Teller will refuse to ap
prove of the leasing of 200,000 acres in
Indian Territory by the Standard Oil
Company, who got the red men to set
tle for quarter-sections and turn over
possession. Barb • wire fences have
been erected around the entiie tract.
Owing to the disturbed condition of
affairs growing out of the potters’
strike at Kast Liverpool, Ohio, the
Mayor has issued a proclamation that
all persons assembling on the streets or
highways, unlawfully congregating in
buildings, or iusulting citizens pursuing
wnrli nill lu* arroaforl
The Cincinnati District Court, by a
majority, has decided that the word
“damn" is profane and vulgar, and sus
tained the action of the Telephone Com
pany in cutting olF the connection of A.
H. Pugh, who used that word in talk
ing over the wire.
William A, Montgomery, 28 years of
age, shot his two children, at Packard
ville, Mass , instantly killing George,
six years old, and probably fatally
wounding Eva, four years old. Mont
gomery and his wife had beeu separated
for some months.
A report has reached Portland, Ore
gon, that three men were lynched at
Priuceville. Crook county, on Monday
night. All that is known of the affair
is that the victims were cowboys of a
desperate character, and were bung on
general principles.
Chief Justice Park decided that the
black Lallots used by the Democrats in
the city election at New IFaven, Conn.,
were illegal and void. This decision
gives the entire municipal government
to the Republoans,
James Clark shot and killed his wife
in a Sixth Avenue baguio at Pittsburgh,
Pa. Clark is a member of a well*
known family, aud bis wife baa for
years borne a bad reputation.
A near relative af General Sherman's
family said the statement that General
Sherman had lately become a member
of the Catholic Church was without
foundation. He had always Ifanejl
toward the Catholic Church, and his
wife and children were members, and
he supposed the General, if he should
join any church, would join that.
He said, however, that he knew cer
tainly that Sherman held no closer rela
tion in that church than he did years
ago, and was not likely to. Another
correspondent says that Sherman, on
being shown the published statement
that he had been converted to Catholi
cism, and the result was in some way
due to the efforts of his wife, who is an
avowed Catholic, says the article is a
lie, and must have been known as such
by its author. It is pure fiction,
manufactured out of whole cloth.
The German Goverumentjis consider
ing a proposal to raise the license tax
on the manufacture of tobacco, so as
to produce a re venue of 93,500,000
marks.
An attempt was made to wreck the
accommodation train from New York
near Meridea, Conn., by a tie placed on
the track.
